scenes.title: The Earth-Moon Stage
scenes.description: Establish the vast scale of cislunar space so students can contextualize the Artemis II mission.
scenes.markdown:
# The Earth-Moon System

## Scale of the System

In this scene, **1 unit = 10,000 km**. The key distances are:

$$d_{\text{Earth-Moon}} = 384{,}400 \text{ km} \approx 38.4 \text{ units}$$

$$R_{\oplus} = 6{,}371 \text{ km} \approx 0.64 \text{ units}$$

$$R_{\text{Moon}} = 1{,}737 \text{ km} \approx 0.17 \text{ units}$$

## Size Comparison

The Moon is only about **27%** of Earth's radius — and at the correct scale of 384,400 km separation, the Moon appears tiny.

$$\frac{R_{\text{Moon}}}{R_{\oplus}} = \frac{1{,}737}{6{,}371} \approx 0.273$$

## How Far Is the Moon?

The Earth-Moon distance is approximately **30 Earth diameters**:

$$\frac{d_{\text{Earth-Moon}}}{2R_{\oplus}} = \frac{384{,}400}{12{,}742} \approx 30.2$$

For comparison, familiar orbital altitudes:

| Orbit | Altitude | Model distance from Earth center |
|---|---|---|
| ISS | 400 km | 0.04 units above surface |
| GPS | 20,200 km | 2.02 + 0.64 = 2.66 units |
| Geostationary | 35,786 km | 3.58 + 0.64 = 4.22 units |
| Moon | 384,400 km | 38.4 units |

## Travel Time

At typical spacecraft speeds (~1 km/s average over coast phase), the journey takes:

$$t = \frac{384{,}400 \text{ km}}{1 \text{ km/s}} \approx 384{,}400 \text{ s} \approx 4.4 \text{ days}$$

Artemis II is planned as a **~10-day mission** — fly out, loop around the Moon on a free-return trajectory, and return to Earth.

## The Free-Return Trajectory

A free-return trajectory is a figure-eight path that uses the Moon's gravity to redirect the spacecraft back toward Earth — no engine burn required at the Moon. It was used by Apollo and is the safety backbone of Artemis II.

## Citations

- NASA Moon Fact Sheet: https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/moonfact.html
- NASA Earth Fact Sheet: https://nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov/planetary/factsheet/earthfact.html
- Wikipedia: Earth-Moon system — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moon
- Artemis II mission overview: https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii/
scenes.prompt: Guide students to appreciate the vastness of cislunar space. Use the scale model to build intuition: the ISS orbit is barely a hair's width above Earth's surface at this scale, while the Moon is 38 units away. Encourage exploration: What if the Moon were closer — how would that change orbital mechanics and travel time? Compare the Earth-Moon distance to everyday experiences — it's like traveling around Earth's equator about 9.5 times. Ask students to estimate how long it would take to drive to the Moon at highway speed (~100 km/h): about 160 days!

scenes.description: SLS Block 1 and Orion — stages, mass ratios, and why staging is necessary
scenes.prompt: You are teaching about the SLS rocket and staging concept. The key insight: rockets must carry their own fuel, and fuel has mass, which requires more fuel — the tyranny of the rocket equation. Staging solves this by dropping empty tanks. Compare the 98m SLS to familiar structures (taller than the Statue of Liberty). Each stage has a specific job: SRBs for raw power at liftoff, core stage for sustained acceleration to orbit, ICPS for precision orbit adjustment. The crew of four sits at the very top in Orion — the only part that comes home.

steps.title: The Full Stack
steps.description: The Space Launch System is the most powerful rocket ever flown. Standing 98 meters tall — taller than the Statue of Liberty — it generates 39 meganewtons of thrust at liftoff.

steps.title: Solid Rocket Boosters
steps.description: Two massive solid rocket boosters provide most of the thrust at liftoff — 32 MN combined. They burn for just 126 seconds before separating at 48 km altitude.

steps.title: Core Stage
steps.description: The orange core stage carries liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen, feeding four RS-25 engines — the same engine design that powered the Space Shuttle. It burns for 8 minutes.

steps.title: ICPS Upper Stage
steps.description: The Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage uses a single RL10 engine. It performs two burns to place Orion into a high Earth orbit before TLI.

steps.title: Orion Spacecraft
steps.description: At the top sits Orion — carrying four astronauts: Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen. Its European Service Module provides propulsion for the lunar journey.

steps.title: Why Staging?
steps.description: About 85% of the rocket's 2.6 million kg liftoff mass is propellant. Staging works by discarding empty tanks — each stage falls away once its fuel is spent, leaving less mass for the next stage to push.

scenes.markdown:
# SLS Block 1 & Orion Spacecraft

The **Space Launch System (SLS) Block 1** is NASA's new super heavy-lift launch vehicle, designed to send the Orion spacecraft and crew to the Moon for the first time since Apollo 17 in 1972.

---

## SLS Block 1 Specifications

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Total height | 98 m (322 ft) |
| Gross liftoff mass | ~2,608,000 kg |
| Liftoff thrust | ~39.1 MN |
| Payload to TLI | ~27,000 kg |
| First flight | November 16, 2022 (Artemis I) |

---

## Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

### Solid Rocket Boosters (×2)
- **Manufacturer**: Northrop Grumman
- **Height**: 54 m each
- **Diameter**: 3.71 m
- **Thrust**: ~16 MN each (32 MN combined)
- **Burn time**: ~126 seconds = **2 min 6 s** = **2.1 min**
- **Propellant**: PBAN solid fuel
- **Separation altitude**: ~48 km
- **Note**: Burn time means the boosters are actively producing thrust for 126 seconds before burnout and separation. Derived from Space Shuttle SRBs, but with 5 segments instead of 4

### Core Stage
- **Manufacturer**: Boeing
- **Height**: 64.6 m
- **Diameter**: 8.4 m
- **Engines**: 4× Aerojet Rocketdyne RS-25
- **Thrust**: ~7.44 MN (combined)
- **Burn time**: ~8 minutes
- **Propellant**: Liquid hydrogen (LH₂) + Liquid oxygen (LOX)
- **Note**: RS-25 engines are heritage Space Shuttle Main Engines

### Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage (ICPS)
- **Manufacturer**: United Launch Alliance (based on Delta Cryogenic Second Stage)
- **Height**: ~13.7 m
- **Diameter**: 5.05 m
- **Engine**: 1× RL10 (11.2 kN vacuum thrust)
- **Propellant**: LH₂ + LOX
- **Mission role**: Two burns — one for circular parking orbit, one for Trans-Lunar Injection (TLI)

---

## Orion Spacecraft

Orion consists of three main elements:

### Crew Module (CM)
- Pressurized cabin for up to 4 astronauts
- Diameter: 5.03 m
- Manufacturer: Lockheed Martin
- Reusable for up to 10 flights

### European Service Module (ESM)
- Provides propulsion, power, thermal control, life support consumables
- Manufacturer: Airbus Defence and Space (ESA contribution)
- Main engine: AJ10 (26.7 kN)
- 33× attitude-control thrusters

### Launch Abort System (LAS)
- Solid-fuel abort motor atop the CM
- Can pull the CM away from a failing rocket in 0.3 seconds
- Jettisoned after max-Q when no longer needed

### Heat Shield
- Avcoat ablative thermal protection
- Must survive 2,760 °C re-entry from lunar return
- Diameter: 5.03 m — largest heat shield ever flown

---

## Artemis II Crew

| Astronaut | Role | Agency |
|-----------|------|--------|
| Reid Wiseman | Commander | NASA |
| Victor Glover | Pilot | NASA |
| Christina Koch | Mission Specialist | NASA |
| Jeremy Hansen | Mission Specialist | CSA |

Artemis II is a **crewed lunar flyby** — the first humans to travel to the Moon since Apollo 17. The mission will not land; it validates Orion's life-support systems with crew aboard on a free-return trajectory around the Moon.

---

## Why Staging Is Necessary

The **Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation** governs all rocketry:

$$\Delta v = v_e \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$

where $v_e$ is exhaust velocity, $m_0$ is initial mass, and $m_f$ is final (dry) mass.

The key insight: **propellant makes up roughly 85% of liftoff mass**. To reach the Moon, you need $\Delta v \approx 12{,}000$ m/s. With a single stage, you would need to carry all the tank structure and engines for the entire journey — an enormous mass penalty.

**Staging solves this** by discarding empty tanks and engines at each phase:
1. SRBs burn out at 48 km → jettisoned (save ~180,000 kg)
2. Core Stage burns out → jettisoned (save ~850,000 kg)
3. ICPS fires twice for orbit insertion and TLI → jettisoned
4. Only Orion continues to the Moon

Each stage shed is dead weight removed — so the remaining propellant pushes a much lighter vehicle, dramatically improving efficiency.

---

## Citations

- [NASA SLS Overview](https://www.nasa.gov/space-launch-system/)
- [Wikipedia — Space Launch System](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Launch_System)
- [Wikipedia — Orion spacecraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orion_(spacecraft))
- [NASA Artemis II Mission Page](https://www.nasa.gov/artemis/artemis-ii/)
- [Tsiolkovsky rocket equation — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tsiolkovsky_rocket_equation)

proof.title: Rocket Thrust & Specific Impulse
proof.goal: Derive rocket thrust from Newton's laws and show that specific impulse $I_{sp} = \frac{v_e}{g_0}$ measures engine fuel efficiency.
proof.prompt: Walk students from Newton's laws to rocket thrust, then to specific impulse. Emphasize that Isp is the rocket engine equivalent of fuel efficiency — higher Isp means more velocity change per kilogram of propellant. Keep the proof focused on the general derivation rather than vehicle-specific comparisons.

steps.label: Newton's Third Law
steps.justification: Every force has an equal and opposite reaction. The rocket pushes exhaust backward; the exhaust pushes the rocket forward.
steps.explanation: A rocket engine works by expelling mass (hot gas) at high speed out the nozzle. By Newton's third law, the exhaust pushing backward creates an equal forward force on the rocket.

nodes.description: Newton's third law: The action force is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the reaction force.
The action force exerted by the rocket on the exhaust gas, pushing it backward.
The negation of the reaction force, indicating it is in the opposite direction to the action force.
The reaction force (thrust) exerted by the exhaust gas on the rocket, pushing it forward.

enrichment.reasoning: The context is a rocket launch, governed by Newton's laws. The domain is classical_mechanics. $F_{action}$ is the force on the exhaust; $F_{reaction}$ is the thrust on the rocket.

steps.label: Newton's Second Law (momentum form)
steps.justification: Force equals the rate of change of momentum — the most general form of Newton's second law.
steps.explanation: For a rocket, both mass and velocity are changing: the rocket gets lighter as it burns fuel, and it accelerates. We need the momentum form, not just $F = ma$.

nodes.description: Net force, $\\vec{F}$, acting on the rocket.
Momentum, $\\vec{p}$, the product of mass and velocity.
Time, the independent variable for the derivatives.
The rate of change of momentum with respect to time, which defines the net force.
The product of the rocket's mass and velocity, which defines its momentum.
The mass of the rocket, which changes as it burns fuel.
The velocity of the rocket.
The rate of change of the rocket's momentum, equal to the net force acting on it.
An equation stating Newton's Second Law: force is the time derivative of momentum.

enrichment.reasoning: The lesson context is a derivation of rocket thrust from Newton's laws, so the domain is `classical_mechanics`. Symbols F, p, m, and v are identified as force, momentum, mass, and velocity.

steps.label: Apply to rocket exhaust
steps.justification: In a small time $dt$, the rocket expels mass $dm$ at a constant exhaust velocity $v_e$ (set by the nozzle design and combustion temperature). The momentum carried away is $dp = dm \cdot v_e$, so the rate of momentum change is $\dot{m} \cdot v_e$.
steps.explanation: A key assumption: the exhaust velocity $v_e$ is approximately constant for a given engine — it depends on the propellant chemistry and nozzle geometry, not on the rocket's speed. This is what makes the equation simple: only the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ varies during a burn.

nodes.label: F_exhaust
nodes.description: Thrust, the force exerted by the rocket's exhaust gases.

nodes.label: p
nodes.description: Momentum of the expelled exhaust gas.

nodes.label: t
nodes.description: Time.
The time rate of change of momentum, which equals force.
Mass flow rate multiplied by exhaust velocity, which equals the rocket's thrust.

The time rate of change of expelled mass, called the mass flow rate.

nodes.label: v_e
nodes.description: Constant velocity of the exhaust gas relative to the rocket.
Mass flow rate multiplied by exhaust velocity, an expression for thrust.

nodes.label: \dot{m}
nodes.description: Mass of the rocket's propellant.
Mass flow rate, $\dot{m}$, the rate at which propellant is consumed.
An equation stating that exhaust force (thrust) equals the rate of change of the exhaust's momentum.

enrichment.reasoning: The context is a lesson on rocket thrust and specific impulse for the SLS rocket, so the domain is classical mechanics. The symbols are unambiguous in this context.

steps.label: Exhaust momentum rate
steps.justification: The thrust $T$ equals the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s of propellant expelled) times the exhaust velocity $v_e$ (speed of the gas leaving the nozzle).
steps.explanation: Each second, $\dot{m}$ kilograms of propellant leave the nozzle at speed $v_e$. The momentum carried away per second is $\dot{m} v_e$, which by Newton's third law equals the thrust force on the rocket.

nodes.description: Relates the rocket's thrust to the product of its propellant mass flow rate and exhaust velocity.
Thrust, the propulsive force generated by the rocket engine by expelling exhaust gas.
The product of mass flow rate and exhaust velocity, which equals the rocket's thrust.
The mass of the rocket's propellant.
Time, the independent variable for the mass flow rate.
The time derivative of propellant mass, representing the mass flow rate $\\dot{m}$.
Exhaust velocity, the speed at which the propellant gas exits the engine nozzle relative to the rocket.

enrichment.reasoning: The context describes rocket propulsion, a topic in classical mechanics. T is thrust, m is propellant mass, and v_e is exhaust velocity.

steps.label: Total impulse from propellant
steps.justification: Impulse is force times time. Since $\dot{m} \cdot t = m_{\text{prop}}$ (total propellant mass burned), the total impulse equals propellant mass times exhaust velocity.
steps.explanation: Impulse measures the total "push" delivered over a burn. A longer burn or higher thrust both increase impulse. This tells us how much momentum the rocket gains from burning all its fuel.

nodes.description: Total impulse, the change in momentum produced by the rocket engine over time.
Thrust multiplied by the burn time gives the total impulse.
Thrust, the force produced by the rocket engine.
Time, specifically the duration of the engine burn.

nodes.subexpr: J = T \cdot t = \dot{m} \cdot v_e \cdot t = m_{\text{prop}} \cdot v_e
nodes.description: An equation showing four equivalent ways to express the total impulse $J$ of the rocket engine.

enrichment.reasoning: The context is a rocket launch simulation, so the domain is `classical_mechanics`. Symbols $J$, $T$, and $v_e$ are identified as impulse, thrust, and exhaust velocity.

steps.label: Impulse per unit weight of propellant
steps.justification: Dividing total impulse by propellant weight ($m_{\text{prop}} \cdot g_0$) gives specific impulse — impulse per unit weight. The propellant mass cancels, leaving $v_e / g_0$.
steps.explanation: The word "specific" means "per unit weight." We divide by weight (not mass) for historical reasons — it gives $I_{sp}$ units of seconds, which is the same in both metric and imperial systems. An $I_{sp}$ of 452 s means one kilogram of propellant produces 452 seconds of 9.81 N thrust.

steps.label: Thrust in terms of Isp
steps.justification: Substituting $v_e = I_{sp} \cdot g_0$ back into the thrust equation.
steps.explanation: This form is useful because $I_{sp}$ is how engines are rated. It separates thrust into three factors: how much propellant leaves each second, how efficiently the engine converts propellant into exhaust speed, and the standard-gravity conversion factor built into the historical definition of specific impulse.

scenes.markdown:
# Cislunar Scale and Nominal Route

This lesson uses a **uniform distance scale** in every direction:

$$1\text{ unit} = 10{,}000\text{ km}$$

So the key mission distances become:

$$R_{\oplus} = 6371\text{ km} \approx 0.6371\text{ units}$$
$$R_{\text{Moon}} = 1737\text{ km} \approx 0.1737\text{ units}$$
$$d_{\oplus\text{-Moon}} = 384{,}400\text{ km} \approx 38.44\text{ units}$$

The route shown here is a **nominal reference trajectory** based on NASA SVS record **5610** and the Artemis II press kit. NASA explicitly notes that the actual trajectory may vary with final launch timing.

The Moon is also **dynamically simulated** rather than pinned in place. In this lesson, the Moon moves on a circular cislunar orbit with mean angular rate

$$\omega_{\text{Moon}} = \frac{2\pi}{27.321661\text{ days}} \approx 0.23\text{ rad/day}$$

and the Artemis II path is timed so the flyby occurs when Orion reaches the moving Moon near mission day 5.

A key mission fact is that Artemis II does **two Earth orbits before translunar injection**. So the route near Earth is not an accidental loop: it represents the planned Earth-orbit setup before Orion heads for the Moon.

That departure is **not** an Earth gravity assist. Orion must execute a real **translunar injection burn** with the service module after the Earth-orbit setup. The gravity-assist style turn happens later at the Moon during the free-return flyby.

## Mission anchors used in this scene

- mission duration: about **10 days**
- closest approach above the Moon: about **4,000 to 6,000 miles**
- communications blackout: about **30 to 50 minutes**
- two Earth orbits before translunar injection
- a thrust-driven translunar injection burn
- free-return path back to Earth

## Sources

- NASA Artemis II press kit: https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit/
- NASA Artemis II mission page: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii/
- NASA SVS API record 5610: https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/api/5610

The route is a nominal NASA-guided free-return model rather than a hand-drawn sketch.

scenes.markdown:
# Earth-Orbit Setup and Departure Burns

NASA's Artemis II profile keeps Orion close to Earth first. The stack enters early Earth orbit, the ICPS shapes the mission into a safe orbit and then a high Earth orbit, Orion separates, and only then does Orion perform the departure sequence.

The mission plan calls for **two Earth orbits before translunar injection**. In this scene, those loops are shown on purpose: they represent the real orbit-raising and departure setup sequence rather than wasted motion.

This part of the mission is **burn-driven**, not a gravity assist. The departure to the Moon happens because Orion performs a **translunar injection burn** after the Earth-orbit setup. In other words, Earth gravity holds the spacecraft in orbit until the burn program changes the orbit enough to send Orion onto the cislunar transfer.

## What does the TLI burn look like?

NASA's prelaunch press kit identified translunar injection as Orion's last major engine firing. Then, on **April 2, 2026**, NASA's Flight Day 2 update reported the actual TLI burn began at **7:49 p.m. EDT** and lasted **5 minutes 49 seconds** using Orion's main engine.

That makes the departure sequence easier to interpret pedagogically:

- the long visible arcs near Earth are coast/orbit-setup phases
- the decisive change in trajectory comes from a relatively short engine burn
- after that burn, Orion enters the outbound coast toward the Moon

## Scaled high-Earth orbit

Using the research brief values:

- perigee radius from Earth center: 
$$r_p = \frac{6371 + 185}{10{,}000} = 0.6556$$
- apogee radius from Earth center:
$$r_a = \frac{6371 + 71{,}645}{10{,}000} = 7.8016$$

That gives a focus-centered ellipse with:
$$a = \frac{r_p + r_a}{2} = 4.2286, \qquad c = a - r_p = 3.5730$$
$$b = \sqrt{a^2 - c^2} \approx 2.2616$$

Those are the numbers used directly in the orbit geometry below.

## Mission stages highlighted here

- ascent
- first Earth orbit and safe-orbit shaping with ICPS
- second Earth orbit and high-Earth-orbit departure setup
- Orion separation from ICPS
- Orion perigee raise burn
- translunar injection burn

## Sources

- NASA Artemis II press kit: https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit/
- NASA Flight Day 2 update, April 2, 2026: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/02/artemis-ii-flight-day-2-crew-houston-poll-go-for-translunar-injection-burn/

scenes.markdown:
# Moon Flyby and Blackout Geometry

This scene is centered on the Moon. Orion's route is shown in **Moon-centered coordinates** so the flyby and blackout geometry are easier to inspect.

## What is modeled here?

- launch-date-dependent closest approach between **4,000 and 6,000 miles** above the lunar surface
- a far-side pass rather than lunar orbit insertion
- the Earth-to-Orion line-of-sight test
- a return route bent back toward Earth
- a moving Earth direction in the Moon-centered frame because the Moon itself is orbiting Earth

## Blackout physics

The blackout is geometric. If the straight line from Earth to Orion passes through the Moon, communications are blocked.

## Sources

- NASA Artemis II press kit: https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit/
- NASA SVS API record 5610: https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/api/5610

scenes.markdown:
# Full Mission Physics Simulator

This capstone scene runs the nominal reference route as one continuous simulation on the same **uniform 10,000 km scale** used in the rest of the lesson.

The Moon is not frozen in place. Its Earth-centered position is updated through the mission using a mean orbital angular rate, so the Earth-Moon geometry and the blackout condition evolve with mission time.

## Mission phases represented

1. ascent
2. Earth-orbit setup
3. outbound cislunar coast
4. far-side lunar flyby
5. return coast
6. entry interface and splashdown

## Burns versus gravity assist

Before leaving Earth, Artemis II performs **two Earth orbits** and then a real **translunar injection burn**. That means the Earth-departure leg is created by thrust, not by a slingshot around Earth.

NASA's Flight Day 2 update on **April 2, 2026** reported the TLI burn lasted **5 minutes 49 seconds**, which is a useful teaching point: a short burn can reshape the entire large-scale route when it is applied at the right orbital point.

Later, at the Moon, the trajectory bends through a **lunar flyby / gravity-assist style turn** that sends Orion onto the free-return path back toward Earth.

## Mission facts shown in the overlays

- mission day and current phase
- distance from Earth and Moon
- flyby-altitude setting
- Moon orbital angle
- blackout condition from line-of-sight geometry
- reentry anchors: **25,000 mph** and **400,000 ft** entry interface

## Source basis

- NASA Artemis II press kit: https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit/
- NASA Artemis II mission page: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii/
- NASA SVS API record 5610: https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/api/5610
- NASA Flight Day 2 update, April 2, 2026: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/02/artemis-ii-flight-day-2-crew-houston-poll-go-for-translunar-injection-burn/
